title: Birmingham Man on One Gallon of Gas
summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby in her Washington home in December 2015, Jennifer Lawson opens on her birth in Fairfield, Alabama, in June 1946, and on a father born in 1899 in Bullock County, the fourteenth of fifteen children, who ran away from the farm at fifteen with a third-grade education to find work in the Birmingham coal mines.

title: Lawson's Repair
summary: Lawson describes how her father taught himself to fix tractors and cars by taking them apart and reassembling them, opened a shop he called Lawson's Repair that mended washing machines and refrigerators, and took her along on calls to mining camps and company towns like Powderly and Bessemer that served Tennessee Coal and Iron.

title: Learning to Wind a Motor
summary: She recalls her father teaching her and her brothers basic repairs, insisting she learn to rewire a motor from coils of copper wire, and an unfound article, said to be in Life magazine, about a Birmingham man who reached Canada on one gallon of gas by running his converted engine on kerosene, the backyard taken up by his repair shop with cars and appliances always under repair.

title: A Modest Bungalow on a Mixed Block
summary: Lawson sketches the economic range of her block, from the doctor and pharmacist a few streets over to the children in the alley without indoor plumbing, her family's modest bungalow in between, her father sometimes paid in barter when the mines struck, and his idea of a good evening reading the encyclopedia at random.

title: Moving the Barrier Up
summary: She describes a totally segregated Fairfield of white-only signs on laundromats and gas stations, the bus that stopped in front of her house with its movable color barrier, and how the neighborhood children, boarding the empty bus, would slide the barrier forward so whites had nowhere to sit, drawing yells from the driver but no serious trouble.

title: A Mother Who Missed the World
summary: Lawson turns to her mother, a former schoolteacher who had attended college and stopped working after the children were born, doing occasional substitute teaching, a woman she calls in retrospect a lonely, depressed figure, in a time when a wife who did not work signaled that the man was a good provider.

title: If He Had a Real Education
summary: She explains that travel let her family escape the constricted segregated world, her father leading excursions to Washington and New York and narrating the Museum of Natural History and Hayden Planetarium with such detail the children groaned, the family joking that with a real education he would have been dangerous, and New York showing race to be arbitrary.

title: The Library Above the Drugstore
summary: Lawson recalls Birmingham removing seats and relegating Black patrons to segregated libraries of worn books, and the women of Fairfield starting their own community library in a room above Dr. Parham's drugstore, where she had her first paying job selling ice cream and helped maintain the shelves Mrs. Parham kept.

title: Talking Across the Street
summary: She remembers her first consciousness of race around age five, a best friend named Teresa Ragusa from the Italian American family that ran the grocery across the street, and the day both sets of relatives told the girls they could no longer play together as elementary school began, leaving them to call across the street from their separate sides.

title: Kerosene on the Wound
summary: Lawson describes adults who did not hide their talk of lynchings, a widely reported case of a Black man kidnapped and castrated in Birmingham whose attackers poured kerosene on the wound and inadvertently saved his life, and her childhood awareness of the Ku Klux Klan and the threatening things whites did to Black people.

title: A Screening at Miles College
summary: She recalls Miles College, the small Black college near her home, hosting a community screening of the banned film Imitation of Life that the segregated Carver Theater would not show, the front-porch discussions of race it sparked across generations, and the theme of passing, with families known to have a relative who had crossed over and disappeared.

title: The Bombed House on Center Street
summary: Lawson describes the wealthier Black families on Birmingham's Center Street, among them Angela Davis's family and an attorney whose ranch-style house was repeatedly bombed, and how urban renewal and a freeway later destroyed that traditionally Black community of nice homes.

title: The Best Peach Cobbler
summary: She recounts Robinson Elementary and the segregated Fairfield schools, poor in resources yet staffed by a mix of barely trained teachers and brilliant ones who in another world might have taught at an Ivy League school, and the lunchroom ladies who, using government commodities, cared enough to make food the neighborhood children would praise.

title: Dare to Struggle, Dare to Win
summary: Lawson describes Fairfield Industrial High, named for the Booker T. Washington vision of industrial training mixed with college prep, its hand-me-down band uniforms and books from the white Fairfield High, and the tough principal E.J. Oliver, who ran it almost as a private school with homilies like dare to struggle, dare to win and inspired alumni reunions that outlasted the school.

title: Just These Little Cotton Pickers
summary: She tells of a nighttime stop by police in Mississippi on a trip west, the officer shining his flashlight into the car and calling her father boy, her father defusing it by saying he was just looking for work with these little cotton pickers in the back, and the children's resentment at watching him be subservient to whites who were also his customers.

title: A Partner of A.G. Gaston
summary: Lawson recalls her father's pride in once partnering with A.G. Gaston, who became one of the first Black millionaires, visits to the gracious Gastons to swim in their pool, the Black papers the men read, the Pittsburgh Courier and Chicago Defender, and her parents voting as Republicans because Alabama's Democratic Party was tied to the Dixiecrats and white citizens' councils.

title: My First Awareness Was the Boycott
summary: She places her first real awareness of the movement at the Montgomery bus boycott when she was about nine, knowing who Rosa Parks was, and describes the roughly hundred-twenty-mile drive to Montgomery on pre-freeway roads through Alabaster, a speed trap where Black travelers feared being stopped by police.

title: This Is Our Land
topic: Summers on the Bullock County Farm
summary: Lawson describes summers on her father's family land in Bullock County, where he showed the children the family's acres and a cold spring, taught her to use a rifle on tin cans, birds, and squirrels, and how she came to dislike shooting and grew fearful of guns after stories of children losing eyes to Christmas BB guns.

title: Glued to the Radio for the Freedom Rides
summary: She recalls following the Freedom Rides as they neared Anniston and Birmingham, then Martin Luther King and the Birmingham demonstrations and the call for students to join, and how Principal Oliver locked the school doors to keep his students from leaving for the protests.

title: We Disobeyed and Went Downtown
topic: Kelly Ingram Park and Mass Meetings
summary: Lawson tells how she and classmates defied the principal, left school, and joined the Kelly Ingram Park demonstrations where some were arrested or hit by firehoses, how her mother had by then died and her father, first upset at the disobedience, came around as a suspension turned to praise once the campaign won, and how mass meetings she attended with him gave her the positive feeling of a community gathered in a crowded church around a serious issue.

part: New York and the Lure of Medicine
title: My Sisters at Hayden Planetarium
topic: A Summer Internship in New York
summary: Lawson explains that in the summer of 1963 she followed the March on Washington from New York rather than attending, holding an internship at Hayden Planetarium arranged by her two older half-sisters, lab technicians there in cancer research, products of a father who, like a proverbial Jewish mother, wanted a doctor in the family.

title: A Cosmopolitan Set
topic: Falling in Love With New York
summary: She describes loving New York at sixteen, commuting from her sister's Queens home to Manhattan, befriending a Swedish nurse and her roommates, a flight attendant and a UN worker, going to plays and experimental theater, and meeting doctors at work, one a Cornell dean who offered to help her into Cornell and its medical school.

title: Like Trying to Eat the Moon
summary: Lawson recounts believing she could be a good doctor yet realizing she did not want to work with the dying, doing computations on a slide rule she had thrilled to master and handing results to doctors who would say a patient need not be done anymore, meaning the person had died, and returning in the summer of 1964 to try cancer research with lab animals and traced tumors, struck when a respected doctor said it had taken twenty years to isolate one small thing, like trying to eat the moon.

title: Why the Whites Broke Up the County
summary: Lawson recalls arriving at Tuskegee, declining the sorority pitch for students talking about desegregating downtown Tuskegee, and curiously seeking out Dr. Gomillion, whose focus on the gerrymandering of Macon County, the whites breaking up the county so Black people could never form a voting majority, intrigued her though it had nothing to do with her schoolwork.

title: You Don't Practice on People
topic: A Scholarship and a Hospital Job
summary: She explains she could attend college only on a full scholarship, got little money from home, and needed to work, taking a job at the Tuskegee hospital on the strength of her Hayden Planetarium experience, then quitting when staff pushed her, untrained, to draw blood from a young woman in labor.

title: Sound Waves in a Pool of Water
summary: Lawson recounts winning her college scholarship, a statewide science-fair prize of full tuition and board to a Black Alabama college, with a project demonstrating sound-wave frequencies vibrating in a pool of water, assembled from parts at a Tandy hobby shop she loved as a still-gadget-oriented tinkerer.

title: A Copied Monopoly Set
summary: She describes her father encouraging the children to design a house expansion with an elevated platform for electric trains and ill-advised skylights, her painting stars and clouds on the playroom walls like a small Sistine Chapel, and copying a friend's Monopoly set by hand down to the box when he refused to buy one, creativity she calls a lasting strength.

title: Beyond the Classroom
topic: A Photo Studio and an Honors Circle
summary: Lawson tells of another Tuskegee job hand-tinting rosy cheeks and lipstick onto black-and-white portraits at what she later learned was a renowned studio documenting the African American community, and of an honors program that let her skip conventional classes, take calculus one-on-one and graduate-level vet-school courses in parasitology and poisonous plants, and through them mingle with professors like the veterinarian Dr. Doris Mitchell, a friend of Odetta with an imported German sports car, in a circle where serious talk of ideas meant talk of race and segregation.

title: This Is Our Group
summary: Lawson recounts beginning to register voters in rural Macon County, meeting Gwen Patton and a chemistry graduate student, and a cohesive Tuskegee student group that later divided, not over strategy but when Mukasa Ricks arrived as a SNCC representative and the group bristled at being told what to do.

title: Engaging the Larger Issues
summary: She explains how, as the campus group insisted on keeping its work parochial and local to downtown Tuskegee, she found that counterproductive and leaned toward SNCC, whose dialogue ranged over the whole country and whose strategy of organizing Black Belt counties promised real change, and reflects that she was predisposed to such work by growing up in Birmingham, joining the high-school demonstrations and living through the church bombing, and by reading in a union town that spoke of John L. Lewis, planting the seed that organized people, not left alone, could have a voice that makes a difference.

title: I Will Go
summary: She tells how, back at Tuskegee after New York in 1964, she answered a call for student volunteers to register voters in Jackson, Mississippi for the November elections, having just missed Freedom Summer, and felt it a conscious decision she made alone, needing no parental or peer validation.

title: A Little Soldier in the Unit
summary: Lawson describes door-to-door canvassing in a part of Jackson where she felt at ease with the work but, shy and introverted, awkward on the social side, mistaking a couple for unmarried and finding a man's flirting off-putting after the tamer New York parties, and feeling like a little soldier in a bigger unit, not privy to the larger strategy but proud to have gone when others called Mississippi too dangerous, reasoning that from the middle of Alabama the state line hardly made it worse, even after Goodman, Schwerner, and Chaney.

title: Still in School When Sammy Was Killed
summary: Lawson describes moving away from the campus group to work with SNCC in Wilcox and Lowndes Counties while still enrolled, the killing of her classmate Samuel Younge Jr., and a group of Tuskegee students, among them Gwen Patton and the Paris brothers, traveling to work in Lowndes and Selma, where she met Phyllis Cunningham of the medical group.

title: Where Do You Stand
summary: Lawson explains how shuttling between Tuskegee, Selma, and Wilcox became untenable and she had to choose whether to give up her paid-for college education for full-time SNCC work with no means of support and nowhere to go between terms, not wanting to return to Fairfield or to her aging father whom she suspected was slipping into dementia, and how, torn between a professor expecting papers and a movement needing her to canvass, she grew depressed with no one to confide in, her brother a supportive but party-loving figure, until she decided to leave for SNCC and convinced herself she could return to medicine later.

title: In Charge of the County
summary: She describes the Tuskegee student group meeting on campus but focusing on Macon County, then joining SNCC and other groups for the Selma to Montgomery march by demonstrating in Montgomery, and the strategy of registering enough Black voters to win pluralities in Black Belt counties like Macon, Wilcox, and Lowndes and put Black people in charge of county government.

title: A Different Kind of Victory
summary: Lawson reflects that the MFDP's Atlantic City challenge was seen as having worked only to a point, that transforming the government of a Black Belt county like Lowndes offered a different path to victory, that Dr. Gomillion was viewed as cautious in pressing the franchise after the Voting Rights Act, and that she still wonders why Wilcox got less effort than Lowndes.

title: Everybody Was a Pettway
summary: She describes Gee's Bend in Wilcox County as a Black supermajority enclave cut off after whites severed the bridge, a planned New Deal community of houses and a community center where everyone, descended from the Pettway plantation, carried the name Pettway, a culturally distinct and isolated place she contrasts with Macon.

title: Billy Clubs on Their Thighs
summary: Lawson recounts James Forman and other SNCC workers at Tuskegee asking students to demonstrate in Montgomery, the group of about forty or fifty marching with picket signs in an oval before the state capitol, the former White House of the Confederacy, state troopers slapping billy clubs against their thighs, as a caravan of unrobed white men and women said to be the Klan drove up the street.

title: Arrest Them All
summary: She describes an officer ordering arrest them all just before the caravan reached the top of the hill, the marchers going limp as their training had taught and being carried to the wagon, and the relief of being arrested rather than left between the troopers' billy clubs and the approaching vigilantes with nowhere to go.

title: Singing Freedom Songs Inside
topic: Five Days in the Montgomery Jail
summary: Lawson recalls about five days in the Montgomery County jail, unafraid because there were so many of them singing freedom songs, the camaraderie hardening as overflowing bathrooms, filth, and inedible food prompted a hunger strike, while more and increasingly diverse people including whites from the North were brought in and local women jailed for other things found the demonstrators a curiosity, then being bailed out without a court appearance, the absurd charge of resisting arrest with no underlying charge dismissed with the help of a movement lawyer she credits with creative strategies.

title: Afros on Auburn Avenue
summary: Lawson tells of a later arrest during the Christmas holidays while working in the Atlanta SNCC office with Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, Karen Edmonds Spellman, and Fay Bellamy Powell, the women closing the office and walking down Auburn Avenue for music and dinner, distinctive in their afros, as Black policemen slowed to a crawl and drove alongside them.

title: To Control the Black Community
summary: She recounts Fay's exasperated retort with an expletive prompting the officers, two of them Vietnam veterans, to pull over and arrest them, and reads the harassment as Black police, intended in part to control the Black community and threatened by civil rights and the rising talk of Black power, seizing an easy chance to show who held authority, dating the incident to about 1967 before turning to organizing in Lowndes County, where she first lived in Wilcox and met with Stokely Carmichael, Ralph Featherstone, Courtland Cox, and Charlie Cobb.

title: You Could See the Stars Looking Up
summary: She describes moving into the Lowndes Freedom House, a shack once belonging to the Jackson family that served as office by day and lodging by night, sleeping in sleeping bags in a building you could see the stars through, warming around a potbelly stove, and being valued as a key worker rather than treated as merely a girl.

title: What Does Freedom Mean
summary: Lawson recalls evenings around the stove reading Frantz Fanon's Black Skin, White Masks and debating freedom, revolution, and power, Ralph Featherstone's conviction that economic empowerment was essential, and later projects toward it: catfish farming in Mississippi, Ed Brown's leadership of Mississippi Action for Community Education, and the Fine Vines blue-jeans garment work.
